sentences.text: Mhm.
No, the Russians always moved ahead and ahead.
So we-- and the SS wanted us not to fall in their hands.
So they keep on moving away from Mauthausen, into-- deeper into Austria, what I think was called Tirol.
It's a-- you know, it's a part of Austria.
And on the way, there was no more-- [LAUGHS] there was no more guards with the SS uniform.
The funny part about it was, the-- the guards were firemen from-- from Vienna, who-- they were actually in their uniform fire-- as a fireman, with a gun over the shoulder.
And those people, I guess, couldn't care less.
You know, they just kept on walking with us on the-- alongside the road.
And I can remember in one-- one instance I-- I looked around.
I was all by myself.
And there in front of me was a little grocery store.
I looked around again, and I said, god, let's try running that grocery store.
And people looked at me, and they-- they start shouting, [SPEAKING GERMAN]!
Don't worry about it!
Take what you want-- anything!
Don't be afraid.
We'll-- we'll give you everything.
Whatever you want, just take it.
And whatever you want to do.
There, there stood, way on the end on the-- on the-- on that grocery store.
And I said to myself, my god, I must look like a devil or something.
I must look really bad.
So I-- I took whatever I could, you know?
How much can you carry?
I took some bread, some-- I don't know-- butter, or-- or a salami-- anything I could find.
And they nodded their head and said [SPEAKING GERMAN]
Take yourself more, if you want, just go ahead-- take it.
All right.
I came out, and nobody else was around again.
And I kept on marching ahead and try to catch up with the rest of the people.
I could have easily run away, you know, in those days.
I don't know.
Maybe what stopped me was that I knew the end is here, anyhow, and why get yourself in something?
And some of the-- some of the SS will come around and say, hey, you're a fugitive.
Bang, bang-- finished.
Maybe I was chicken.
It's possible.
[CHUCKLE] Well, I-- I know people-- there was-- there was hardly anything to eat.
I seen people take-- you know, snails, already, came out.
It was to a spring.
So they had a little-- they had some cans-- conservation cans.
And took the snails, took them out of the shell, put them in there, and heat them up with-- they made little fires, in the evening, you know, and then ate them.
It's awkward, you know, it's terrible.
Oh, they-- they took anything from-- whatever there was green-- green from the lawns and anywhere and put them in and started cooking and eating.
Finally we got into a camp-- a little camp.
Wasn't very big.
And it had about, oh, three, four barracks, that's all.
No beds, only barracks.
And--
That was called-- that was Gunskirchen.
I mean, the real little town was outside the camp, right across the-- the highway, on the other side of the highway.
Gunskirchen.
It was Gunskirchen.
That-- that's known.
Anybody you ask in-- in-- in Austria, they know.
Gunskirchen, yeah, we know where is Gunskirchen.
But what got me-- that this unterscharfuhrer-- Ruckas was his name.
He was there.
And I looked at him, and he was the only SS around.
And I guess he-- he knew that the thing is going to come to an end, but he was still mean and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killing people or-- or trying to keep the-- the Hitler idea up.
I can remember that so many dead people were laying around.
We had to carry them-- they made a big, you know, a hole to throw them in.
People were nothing but skeletons and-- and bones.
And then there was one fellow-- he-- he had him walking.
And him-- he with his luger-- onto-- on the back on his head.
And he-- he told him to keep on going-- go!
And the man knew it's gonna be his end.
And he begged-- please, Herr Unterscharfuhrer, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Don't, don't!
Well, it didn't do no good.
One shot.
And I can-- I can never forget.
It's like when you take a-- a sack of sugar or anything.
You hold it up, and let it go.
Bang.
He dropped.
Just like that.
And then somebody pushed him in, and that was it.
And then, at night, we-- we had to lay on the floors.
In the beginning, I did.
But what happened was, everybody-- once you get up and you try to get back to the same place, there wasn't anything-- place to go back.
And people did there-- there was no toilets, so they-- they did whatever they did in-- in-- the-- in those little cans and sprayed the thing over you.
I said, no more-- not me.
Like a barrack looks is-- there's rafters up on top.
That's only.
And I climbed up here, and I laid up there.
Was about that wide.
And I laid up there and put my-- my back towards the thing-- towards the-- you know-- and nobody bothered me.
And I didn't have to go through whatever happened down-- down below.
That only lasted a few days.
And one morning, I said-- [LAUGHS] I look up-- I wake up, I look down.
Everybody's gone.
And I said, wow, what's going on?
Here.
I go down.
And I ran to the gate.
And here, there was about three, four German soldiers laying there, dead.
And the jeep stood there, with Americans.
I think it was the Eighth Army.
And they waved to me-- come on, come on!
Get out!
And then I seen-- that's it.
So I did kiss the ground.
Really.
And, from there, I walked out of the camp, out of this-- it wasn't in the woods.
And I come to a highway.
Across the highway was a train station.
And there was a big corrugated-- corrugated place with-- no, they call that a depot.
You know, from merchandise, to put in.
And two or three MPs standing in front of it, with their guns and let-- would let-- would let nobody in.
And the people outside, the-- the-- the haftlinge-- you know, the-- the-- the prisoners-- pleaded-- please, please, let us in, let us in!
And they knew-- the-- the MP knew what was inside.
It was piled up with sugar.
Finally, they opened the thing up.
And everybody just ran in there, ripped open the sugar, and, with their hands, and ate the thing, without thinking-- anything.
And also there was a cereal in there, too.
And I looked at it, and I-- I don't know, my brain was still working.
So I went in there.
I took-- I don't know how, but I took sugar, and I took cereal, and I walked away from there.
And I walked down the street in one of the homes, on the end.
And I come there-- knock on the door.
Since I was-- I was speaking German, she understood-- the woman understood very well.
I said, I have this.
I give you some sugar.
Whatever you want, I give it to you, but please, cook this with me-- for me.
With milk and sugar, you know, and the cereal, so I have something to eat.
I have diarrhea, and I don't want-- I don't want to eat anything else-- just so I have something in my stomach.
OK, OK, OK, naturally.
She did.
She did it.
I walked away from her, and I went on the highway.
And every, I don't know, every 20, 40 feet, I sat down.
I couldn't walk.
I was just exhausted.
I come into-- Wels was the name of that town, the bigger town.
And I-- everybody walked to the same place, and I walked after them.
Where was-- where we went to was a hospital.
There were-- there were nuns who took care of the hospital, and doctors were in there.
And the MP, again, stood outside.
Nobody could get in.
I said, oh my god, now what?
I want to get in!
So I walk around-- from the back.
And here I seen a couple nuns-- one or two-- and I hollered at them, please, sister, help me!
I'm sick!
I have diarrhea!
Let me in!
We can't, we can't!
Well, I begged so long, and finally they said, OK, OK, come on-- quick.
Take everything off.
All what you have on.
Nothing.
We won't-- not even the shoes.
Nothing.
So they got me in.
They put me in.
And then they put me down in the-- in the cellar.
And that's when they started to-- had-- they had a whole [INAUDIBLE] full of disinfectant-- some brown stuff-- I don't know-- whatever.
Everybody had to go in there.
And they scrubbed you here and there and out and gave you a towel.
Something you keep, you know, covered up.
And they put you on a-- on a stretcher.
And it took another day.
Finally, I ended up upstairs in a regular-- regular bedroom-- a regular room, with white pillows.
Never seen that-- I haven't seen that for, you know-- for years.
White pillows, white sheets.
I said, oh my god.
And, as I turned around-- my hair was not cut, really.
It was cut, but-- there was lice all over the pillow.
So the doctor came, then, the next day and told me, listen to me.
You're fine.
You're OK.
You only have diarrhea.
But remember, everybody in this room has typhus.
You better get out before you catch it.
So I did.
I walked away.
And I walked in the street and had to go into a displaced-person camp.
I couldn't-- you know-- I was just not fit enough to say, now we are free!
Let me go!
Why do you have to be in a camp again?
Enough camp!
I ran away again.
Who-- who took you to the DP camp?
You walked to it?
I walked, and I think soldiers picked me up on the street--
And took you to that--
--Americans, and put me there.
OK?
And then, and [INAUDIBLE] I was in there.
I ran away.
I-- I couldn't just-- I could not comprehend it.
There was a-- all my thinking was, how to get back to Palestine-- how to get back to my family, who had a-- you know, in-- in-- in Haifa had a little store.
And I knew the address.
[INAUDIBLE] Halutz 46 or 47.
That was-- that was my-- my father's brother and my-- my-- and sister and my mother's brother, who also were there, who came from that [INAUDIBLE] in Germany, who could-- could leave, earlier.
That time, when they left, I was told that-- told my father they wanted to take me with them.
So my father said, no, you ain't gonna take him.
Oh.
I could have-- you know, I could have been with them. 'Cause they-- they-- they said, it's fine, we will take him.
He-- we'll tell them he's one of us-- our-- our children.
But he wouldn't do that.
He wouldn't let me go.
Yeah, right, yes.
With all--
--all the might.
So I ran away all the time.
And they brought you back.
And they always brought me back.
They-- no, we didn't do anything.
We just sat around, waiting.
That's all.
And one time-- I-- I said that before-- I was drinking water from a can-- a conversation-- conservation can-- and was drinking water.
I don't know where the water got from.
And I was drinking.
As I was drinking, the guy who was the head of this hit me, and he got me that mark, right there.
And blood come running out.
Because I think that the water was contaminated, too.
That-- you know?
So he didn't want me to get sick and make others sick.
That was a displaced-person thing.
And then-- Did anything else happen in that camp that you can tell us about?
No-- well, they would put me, once, in a-- in a small, I don't know, four by five-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do they call it--
A cell-- a four, five-- cell, you know?
Just-- couldn't-- you couldn't lay down.
You had to stand up.
They do that to-- to in order to-- to get me to listen to them and get normal.
I don't think I was normal.
I was so, you know, enraged.
But then, from there, I-- I walked, and I got to Salzburg.
And Then, from that time on, I felt that-- I-- every time I was laying on one side, this side hurt bad.
When I laid on the other side, and that one-- the other side hurt.
Well, here we-- we met the first legion who were British, under Palestine legion, under the British command.
They had 'Palestine' written on their-- on their jackets, but they were Jews from Palestine, those days, but under British command.
And they took us.
They knew we were Jews, and they wanted to-- what they wanted-- they wanted to get us to Palestine, to help, to get out.
No, no, no.
They took us-- it was on my birthday-- July the 15th, 1945.
I travelled over the Brenner Pass with trucks and ended up in Venice.
You know, by the-- by the Adriatic Sea?
There, I met somebody I was together with in-- in-- in Cologne.
Hugo [?
Herzog ?] was his name.
And I told him, Hugo, please, I want you to write a letter to my family in Israel, in Haifa, that I'm alive-- that I'm there, and I'm coming.
Because I didn't have any-- anything to write with.
I think he did.
But after that, I ended up in Bologna and, from there, went into Reggio Emilia.
Then I was-- I was ill, and I was-- I was treated.
And-- and-- a doctor treated me, with my illness, being-- having pleurisy.
And one day, in-- in-- in the city, he put me up on this table, and he said, take off the shirt.
Took a needle that long and went in between the ribs.
And took-- without anaesthesia-- anything-- and took-- took the fluid out.
And then, after that, I was, like, a new man.
Or a kid.
I recovered, yeah.
From there, the British had this-- it was under British control, that Reggio Emilia.
And every time the British came in, the officers and the sisters there, the nurses, they pointed to me.
You know him?
He was nothing.
He was a skeleton.
Look at him now.
I was like that.
I was eating.
I was eating maybe too much.
Didn't do anything, just laying around.
At night you used to go to the movies.
That's all.
Or visit other places who are-- who were-- what should I say?
I was-- I was-- I was a virgin.
So I had to go try it-- try out how it feels to be with a woman.
[LAUGHS] So I went there, too, so.
And after that, we were told-- we went to another city, not far away.
And I don't remember the name.
But one day we were there, we were told, get ready.
Take your shower now.
And take whatever you can.
And you're going to go to a special place.
Nobody ask.
They wouldn't answer you.
So here comes about four or five trucks-- the tarpaulin down.
And they pushed us in.
Were you worried?
No, not really.
I wasn't worried, no.
There were-- there were Jews, you know, from Palestine.
They-- they showed what they are.
But they told us, you're going-- you know, you-- you're going to a trip, but we cannot tell you now where.
But you don't have to worry about it.
Everything is going to be fine.
It's no more Auschwitz.
[LAUGHS] So--
Huh?
They took us to Genoa-- that was right on the French-Italian Riviera-- in the middle of the night.
And here, we got out, and there was a little boat on the-- on the-- on the breakwater.
And it was, I think, a 9,000-ton.
That's all.
It was a little nothing.
And we all got in there.
And we finally got underway.
The-- the crew was all Italian.
And inside, I don't know, there was five, six beds-- you know, I mean, cots.
Bunks.
We were laying in there.
And went on our way.
It was a beautiful day.
I can remember going through-- going by Etna, the-- the [INAUDIBLE], the Strait of Messina.
[LAUGHS] And I can never forget that.
It was such a clear day.
And we kept on going.
And, as we come to the point-- you could see Haifa, or you could see Israel-- at that time, Palestine-- you could see the-- the mountains in the back.
And-- oh, yeah, that's it.
And as we just approached, two Spitfires came right over the boat.
And, you know, but we looked.
And another half an hour, a-- a cruiser was right alongside of us-- British.
And then the people who-- from the-- they were already from the Haganah-- the Jews who-- who-- brought us to-- to this boat-- they took creates-- threw them overboard.
The crates were full of the ammunition and full of guns and full of-- you know, ready for the war, what eventually would happen in Israel.
And so we finally came to Haifa.
And I holler down-- people down-- and said, please, call-- call-- tell [INAUDIBLE], tell my uncle and my aunt that I'm here, and I'm-- I'm-- I'm alive.
And I'm from here.
I don't know where I'm going, but-- They brought us to Atlit-- what was a famous camp and is, today, still-- and kept us there.
And-- you know, they make sure that we are clean, that there is no-- no sickness on us to-- to-- to be--
--contagious to somebody else.
I know-- one thing don't-- I always won't forget.
When finally my-- my cousin-- she was a girl cousin-- [?
Tovah ?] is her name.
She picked me up, and I had papers already they gave us, you know, that we are legal in Palestine.
She passed away, too, long ago-- well, 20 years or so ago, on breast cancer, but-- When I came, and I met my uncle-- Uncle Max.
He was-- my-- I was his godchild.
And we met, and I was going to kiss him, and he wouldn't.
He was afraid that I had some sickness and that I had, you know, brought with me.
So he stood away.
I said, Uncle Max, don't worry about it!
I'm-- I'm OK-- I'm clean!
They wouldn't let me out without it.
OK, then, later he understood.
And-- and I guess he was OK.
And, from there on, I lived with them.
And what came after that?
Oh, we met-- everybody came and-- and-- and looked me over.
People came.
I know a-- a woman came to me, and I was together with the man.
I don't remember what his name was.
He was a very heavy man.
And he was in Auschwitz with me.
I don't know how he got there.
And she asked me, what happened to-- you know, you knew him?
I said, yes, I knew him.
I said.
Well, what happened to him?
Well, what can I say?
He-- he-- he couldn't take it.
A man with that much weight-- how could he survive on that little food we had?
So he just got smaller and smaller.
And, you know, he-- he was just not anymore available-- he couldn't work anymore, so they shipped him through the chimney, like they say.
And you had to tell that to--
And I had to tell her that, yes.
--your family.
Oh, she-- she thanked me.
She wanted to know, naturally.
Well, I found-- I found out about it.
And-- and naturally it was like a shock for me.
I knew he never showed, he never heard anything.
And-- and it made me angry that a man like him, who was fighting for the German 'fatherland,' so to speak of-- he was a-- he was a-- a--
A veteran?
Well, he was a-- a field-artillery man.
He-- he handled field artillery.
You know, he was a big, tall man.
And he-- he had to go and bite in this apple, like they say and had to be killed.
And-- and Hitler wouldn't care, even he was a veteran.
Well, I knew there was no-- no more--
--contact.
I didn't hear anything.
And-- and-- and it was natural to assume that he-- he died.
And how?
Well, who knows?
All kinds of ways how they tried to get rid of us.
Well--
I was in Israel till-- well, then came the war of liberation, the-- the-- in 1948.
Hmm?
Independence or liberation-- independence.
I joined.
And I-- I went through the-- one time they had me in Akko, with-- with machine guns to-- you know, to shoot.
Akko was on the border, on the-- right on the-- on the beach.
And we-- we had to practice shooting with machine guns-- with German machine guns, or Czechoslovakian machine guns.
We would practice there.
And we were ready to go to battle.
And some of the-- some of the guys said, do we have to go?
So the commander said, oh-- you-- you will not go.
Forget it.
You are not ready for that.
We won't send you.
You cannot be a real soldier if you worry about what's going to happen to you.
So-- This is the end--
It was the end, there, [INAUDIBLE].
My cousin--
Oh, OK.
OK.
